purpose: Draft (inactive) question objects derived from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-6 batch -- a completed, handwritten 'Review Quiz #6' (Water Survival, SHLSOS Course #549), 22 fill-in-the-blank questions across 5 pages. These are NOT part of src/content/course.json's live "questions" array and will not appear in the app until explicitly promoted.
activationRule: Do not add any of these questions to course.json unless its verificationStatus has been changed away from 'draft-unverified'.
duplicateCheckNotes: Cross-referenced against all 250 live questions in src/content/course.json before drafting -- see the fuller duplicateCheckNotes in vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json for specifics. Dropped as duplicates: Q6 (Type I face-up design, duplicates q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false), Q18 (side-bench air tanks, duplicates q-quiz1a-side-bench-buoyancy-location and q-ch2-airtanks-vh), Q19 (sealing clamp, duplicates q-quiz1a-sealing-clamps), Q20 (tricing pendant timing, duplicates q-gloss-tricing), Q22 (windward launch disadvantage, substantially given already in q-quiz1a-windward-manual-launch's misconception text). Questions 7, 8, 9 were left blank by the student. Two near-duplicate pairs within this quiz were merged: Q3+Q4 (liferaft cold/tropical climate adaptation) and Q13+Q16 (condensation collection).

questions.prompt: If there are a number of liferafts scattered about after their ship sinks, what should be done?

choices.text: Group them up and tie them together
Keep them spread apart to cover more search area
choices.misconception: Spreading rafts apart makes each one individually harder to spot and reduces survivors' ability to share resources and support each other.

choices.text: Each raft should deploy its own sea anchor and drift independently
choices.misconception: Ignores the value of grouping rafts together for visibility and mutual aid.

choices.text: Nothing -- rafts should be left exactly as they land in the water
choices.misconception: Passively leaving rafts scattered misses a real, actionable step survivors can take.

hints: Think about visibility to searchers and the ability of survivors in different rafts to help each other.
questions.explanation: If a number of liferafts are scattered about after their ship sinks, survivors should group them up and tie them together -- improving visibility to search and rescue and allowing mutual support between rafts.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-1.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 1 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: After landing on a beach with your liferaft, what should you do with the liferaft?

choices.text: Carry it past the high tide line
Leave it at the waterline for easy relaunching if needed
choices.misconception: A rising tide could carry the raft back out to sea or damage it against the shore if left near the waterline.

choices.text: Deflate it immediately and discard it
choices.misconception: The raft remains valuable shelter and equipment after landing -- it shouldn't be discarded.

choices.text: Push it back out to sea once everyone is ashore
choices.misconception: The raft is still useful survival equipment on land and shouldn't be sent back out to sea.

hints: Think about what the tide will do to anything left too close to the water's edge.
questions.explanation: After landing a liferaft on a beach, it should be carried past the high tide line -- protecting it (and its continued usefulness as shelter and equipment) from being carried off or damaged by a rising tide.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-1.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 2 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A liferaft's setup should differ depending on climate. What's the difference between the proper setup in higher (colder) latitudes versus tropical waters?

choices.text: In higher latitudes, inflate the floor and close the doors to lessen cold water radiation; in tropical waters, open the canopy entryways and deflate the floor for ventilation
The setup is identical regardless of climate -- floor and doors are never adjusted
choices.misconception: Ignores that climate-specific adjustments to the floor and doors are a real, deliberate survival practice.

choices.text: In higher latitudes, open the doors for ventilation; in tropical waters, inflate the floor for insulation
choices.misconception: Reverses the two climate-specific setups.

choices.text: The floor should always be deflated and the doors always closed, regardless of climate
choices.misconception: Applies one climate's setup universally, ignoring the need to adapt to conditions.

hints: Think about which direction you want heat to move in each climate -- keeping cold out in higher latitudes, versus letting heat and humidity escape in the tropics.
questions.explanation: In higher (colder) latitudes, inflating the floor and closing the doors lessens cold water radiation into the raft. In tropical waters, the opposite adjustments help: opening the canopy entryways and deflating the floor improve ventilation and reduce heat/humidity buildup.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-1.jpg (Review Quiz #6, questions 3 and 4 of 22, combined -- a natural compare-and-contrast pairing of cold-climate and tropical-climate liferaft setup, merged into one question rather than drafted as two separate ones); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Besides basic flotation, what items should a lifejacket or personal flotation device have on it?

choices.text: A light, a whistle, reflective patches, and the name of the vessel
A GPS transponder and a two-way radio
choices.misconception: Overstates what's actually required -- these are far more elaborate than the standard items on a PFD.

choices.text: Only a whistle -- no light or reflective material is required
choices.misconception: Understates the required items -- a light and reflective material are also required, not just a whistle.

choices.text: Nothing beyond the flotation material itself

hints: Think about what helps rescuers both see and hear a survivor, plus what helps identify which vessel they came from.
questions.explanation: A lifejacket or PFD should have a light, a whistle, reflective patches, and the name of the vessel on it -- helping rescuers locate, hear, and identify survivors.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-1.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 5 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json. Note: the already-live q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false lists 'reflective tape, a whistle, and strobe light' as a true statement about Type I life preservers in passing (as a distractor's descriptive text, not that question's tested answer) -- this question directly tests the fuller item list as its own fact, adding 'name of vessel' as new content.

questions.prompt: What is the safest method of entering the water from a sinking ship if there is an oil fire on the surface?

choices.text: Enter from the stern or bow, on the windward side
Enter from amidships, on the leeward side
choices.misconception: The leeward side is where wind would blow burning oil and smoke toward the survivor -- the opposite of safe.

choices.text: Enter from wherever is most convenient -- fire location doesn't affect the choice
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, deliberate safety consideration -- the fire's position relative to wind direction matters a great deal.

choices.text: Wait until the fire burns out before entering the water at all
choices.misconception: Waiting isn't a safe option when abandoning a sinking ship -- entry point and side matter, not delay.

hints: Think about which side of the ship the wind would blow burning oil and smoke away from.
questions.explanation: The safest way to enter the water from a sinking ship with an oil fire on the surface is from the stern or bow, on the windward side -- keeping the wind blowing the fire and smoke away from the survivor rather than toward them.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-2.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 10 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: When trying to swim to a shore where there is a strong current, what angle should you swim relative to the current?

choices.text: 45 degrees out of (across) the current
Straight against the current, head-on
choices.misconception: Fighting a strong current head-on wastes energy rather than working with it at an angle.

choices.text: Directly with the current, ignoring the shore's direction
choices.misconception: Swimming purely with the current abandons the goal of actually reaching shore.

choices.text: 90 degrees, directly across the current with no angle toward shore
choices.misconception: A full 90-degree crossing doesn't make progress toward shore -- a 45-degree angle balances crossing the current with heading toward land.

hints: Think about balancing two goals at once: not exhausting yourself fighting the current, and still making progress toward shore.
questions.explanation: When swimming to shore against a strong current, swim at a 45-degree angle out of (relative to) the current rather than straight against it -- conserving energy while still making progress toward land.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-3.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 11 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What steps should be taken to maneuver a lifeboat and make way while avoiding broaching in heavy seas?

choices.text: Keep the bow to the seas, and use a sea anchor if necessary
Keep the stern to the seas, running directly downwind
choices.misconception: Running with the stern to the seas increases the risk of broaching, not avoids it.

choices.text: Turn broadside to the seas for a more stable ride
choices.misconception: Being broadside to heavy seas is precisely the broaching risk this question is asking how to avoid.

choices.text: Maintain maximum speed regardless of the boat's heading relative to the seas
choices.misconception: Speed alone doesn't prevent broaching -- heading relative to the seas, and using a sea anchor if needed, is what matters.

hints: Broaching happens when a boat turns unexpectedly broadside to the seas -- think about which heading avoids that.
questions.explanation: To maneuver a lifeboat and make way while avoiding broaching in heavy seas, keep the bow to the seas, and use a sea anchor if necessary to help maintain that heading.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-3.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 12 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of collecting condensation in a liferaft, and how is it typically done?

choices.text: It's collected -- with a sponge or a t-shirt -- as a source of drinking water
It's collected to keep the raft's interior dry, then discarded
choices.misconception: Discards the actual value of the collected condensation -- it's kept and used as drinking water, not thrown away.

choices.text: It's collected only to test whether the canopy has a leak
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated diagnostic purpose -- the real purpose is obtaining drinking water.

choices.text: Condensation isn't worth collecting in a liferaft

hints: Think about how scarce fresh water is in a liferaft, and how even small amounts matter.
questions.explanation: Condensation inside a liferaft is collected -- using a sponge or a t-shirt to soak it up -- as a supplementary source of drinking water, since fresh water is scarce for survivors.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-3.jpg and 2026-07-07_065906-4.jpg (Review Quiz #6, questions 13 and 16 of 22, combined -- question 13 asked the purpose, question 16 asked the method with an additional tool; merged into one question rather than drafted as two overlapping questions); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Your ship is on course 090°T and someone falls overboard on the starboard side. What maneuver does the mate on watch execute, and what's the ship's heading after its first phase?

choices.text: A Williamson Turn -- turning toward the side the person fell until the heading changes 60 degrees from the original course, reaching 150°T
A Williamson Turn -- turning away from the side the person fell, reaching 030°T
choices.misconception: Turns away from (rather than toward) the side the person fell, and applies the heading change in the wrong direction.

choices.text: An immediate full stop, with no turn at all
choices.misconception: A full stop alone doesn't reposition the ship to recover the person -- the Williamson Turn's heading change is the actual maneuver.

choices.text: A Williamson Turn, but the heading change is 180 degrees immediately, with no intermediate phase

hints: The Williamson Turn's first phase changes the ship's heading by a specific number of degrees from its original course, toward the side the person fell -- not straight to the reciprocal course.
questions.explanation: For a man overboard, the mate on watch executes a Williamson Turn -- turning toward the side the person fell (starboard, here) until the ship's heading has changed 60 degrees from its original course: 090°T + 60° = 150°T. (The full maneuver continues from there to reach the reciprocal heading, retracing the ship's track back to the person's position, but this question covers the well-documented first phase.)
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-3.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 14 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json. Note: the student's answer ('Williamson turn 60°' / '90 = 150°') documents only the turn's first phase; this question is scoped to match exactly what the source supports, not the full maneuver's later steps.

questions.prompt: What is the proper procedure for jumping into the water when abandoning ship?

choices.text: Cross your arms and legs, and make sure there's no debris below before jumping
Keep your arms and legs spread wide for a bigger splash landing
choices.misconception: A wide, spread-limb landing increases the risk of injury on impact -- crossing the arms and legs is the safer technique.

choices.text: Dive in headfirst to minimize impact
choices.misconception: A headfirst dive risks serious head/neck injury, especially into unknown water conditions below.

choices.text: Jump immediately without checking what's below, to save time

hints: Think about protecting your limbs on impact, and checking for hazards in the water below before you commit to jumping.
questions.explanation: When jumping into the water to abandon ship, cross your arms and legs to protect them on impact, and make sure there's no debris (or people) below before jumping.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-4.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 15 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json. Note: distinct from the already-live 'preferred jump height of 15 feet or less' fact -- this question covers body position and a pre-jump safety check, not height.

questions.prompt: What does a continuous ringing of the general alarm bell (no less than 10 seconds), combined with a continuous blast of the ship's whistle (more than 10 seconds), indicate?

choices.text: Fire or a general emergency
Commence lowering the lifeboats
choices.misconception: The lowering signal is a single short blast, not a continuous bell-and-whistle combination.

choices.text: Man overboard
choices.misconception: Not the signal associated with this specific continuous bell-and-whistle combination.

choices.text: Boat drill dismissal
choices.misconception: Dismissal from boat stations uses a different signal (three short blasts of the whistle), not this continuous combination.

hints: This is the ship's first/general alarm -- distinct from the separate signal used specifically to call the crew to boat stations.
questions.explanation: A continuous ringing of the general alarm bell for no less than 10 seconds, combined with a continuous blast of the ship's whistle for more than 10 seconds, indicates fire or a general emergency -- the ship's first alarm signal, distinct from the separate seven-short-blasts-plus-one-long signal used specifically to call the crew to boat stations.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-4.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 17 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What oar command is given to take all the way off a lifeboat that's moving through the water?

choices.text: "Hold water all"
"Way enough"
choices.misconception: 'Way enough' is the command to finish the stroke and lift the oars from the rowlocks -- a different action from stopping the boat's way through the water.

choices.text: "Backwater all"
choices.misconception: 'Backwater all' reverses the oar stroke to row backward, rather than bracing the oars to stop the boat's forward way.

choices.text: "Out oars"
choices.misconception: 'Out oars' moves the oars from the side benches to the gunwales, an entirely different action from stopping the boat.

hints: This command braces the oar blades in the water to act as a brake, distinct from the commands that reposition or stow the oars.
questions.explanation: "Hold water all" is the oar command given to take all the way off a lifeboat that's moving through the water -- distinct from 'Way enough' (finishing the stroke and stowing oars), 'Backwater all' (reversing), and 'Out oars' (repositioning oars from the side benches to the gunwales).
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-5.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 21 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json
